title: Glamorgan sausages
subtitle: Welsh cheese and leek sausages, breadcrumbed and fried
excerpt: The traditional Welsh meatless sausage: Caerphilly or Cheddar cheese mixed with leeks, breadcrumbs, and egg, shaped into sausages, coated in more breadcrumbs, and fried until golden. Crisp outside, molten and savoury within.
sourceNotes: Glamorgan sausages are documented in Welsh culinary tradition from the nineteenth century. George Borrow, Wild Wales (1862), mentions Glamorgan sausages as a local speciality. Bobby Freeman, First Catch Your Peacock (1980), gives the definitive modern recipe and traces its history as a Welsh farmhouse dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked after shaping and crumbing. Fry from frozen over medium heat.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and refrigerate up to 12 hours ahead. Coat just before frying.

content.text: Glamorgan sausages are among the finest cheese dishes in British cooking. The combination of Welsh cheese and leek is natural and well-established; the breadcrumb coating gives the exterior a satisfying crunch while the inside stays soft and intensely savoury. They are not a meat substitute but a distinct dish that stands on its own merits.

Caerphilly is the traditional Welsh cheese here: it is crumbly, slightly salty, and has a clean flavour that does not overwhelm the leek. A mature Cheddar is an acceptable alternative with a stronger, sharper result. Dry mustard powder in the mix adds a background warmth without being identifiable as mustard.

items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, finely chopped
1 whole, 1 yolk (reserve the white for coating)

content.text: Combine {{caerphilly-cheese}} of grated cheese, {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of fresh breadcrumbs, finely chopped leek, the whole egg and one yolk, mustard powder, chopped parsley, and thyme if using in a bowl. Season well. Mix until the mixture holds together when pressed. If too dry, add a splash of water; if too wet, add more breadcrumbs.
Divide into 8 equal portions and shape each into a sausage about 8 cm long. Roll in the reserved egg white, then in panko breadcrumbs, pressing to coat. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up.

Heat a little sunflower oil and butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Fry the sausages for 3 to 4 minutes on each side until golden all over and heated through — turn gently as they are fragile. Serve hot with a tomato salad, pickles, or mustard.

items.symptom: Sausages are falling apart during frying
items.cause: Mixture was too loose, or not chilled before cooking
items.fix: Chill the shaped sausages for at least 30 minutes. If the mixture feels too wet, add more fresh breadcrumbs and reshape.

items.cause: Too much breadcrumb relative to cheese
items.fix: The cheese is the binder and the flavour: do not reduce it. If the mixture seems dry before shaping, add a splash of water rather than reducing the cheese.

items.symptom: Outside is browning before the inside is hot
items.fix: Medium heat is correct. The sausages need time to heat through without burning the crumb.

content.text: Glamorgan sausages are named after the historic county of Glamorgan in South Wales, where Caerphilly cheese production was historically centred. George Borrow mentioned them with approval in Wild Wales (1862), which gives some indication of their local status by the mid-nineteenth century. Bobby Freeman's First Catch Your Peacock (1980) brought them back into wider British awareness after a period of obscurity, and they have since been recognised as one of the most distinctive Welsh dishes: a meatless sausage predating the vegetarian cooking movement by well over a century, valued for its own flavour rather than as a substitute.
